key_insight: Localized shell defects interact over long distances through butterfly-shaped post-buckling deformation, so the deepest defect does not always trigger buckling.
engineering_relevance: Cylinder stability design must account for defect populations and shell length-to-thickness ratio H/t, not just the largest imperfection or R/t alone.
method: High-fidelity finite-element simulations of axially compressed cylindrical shells with single, paired, and edge-near localized defects
Stochastic simulations with randomly distributed defect amplitudes sampled from a log-normal distribution
Parameter mapping of defect sensitivity, defect-edge coupling, and defect-defect interactions
evidence_signal: The simulations show a statistical size effect: as the mean number of defects increases, the mean knockdown factor decreases and its variability decays exponentially.
watch_item: The study’s knockdown factors remain above historical experimental values, so the Gaussian dimple model may still underpredict the severity of real cylindrical-shell imperfections.
paper_title: The Butterfly Defect Effect in Cylindrical Shell Buckling: Non-localized Interactions of Localized Imperfections
